content: Don’t Just Make The Page Fast, Prove It Is Fast 10 min read Just now -- Press enter or click to view image in full size When frontend engineers talk about performance, the conversation usually starts with bundle size, lazy loading, image compression, or Lighthouse scores. Those things matter, but they are not the full story. A page can have a clean Lighthouse report and still feel slow to real users. A hero image can load quickly in your office Wi-Fi test and still be late for users on mobile networks. A React page can render fast locally but delay the actual above-the-fold content in production because hydration, fonts, API calls, or third-party scripts block the final paint. That is why monitoring Largest Contentful Paint, or LCP, is important. LCP answers a user-centered question: How long did it take for the most important visible content to appear? For most product pages, that “important visible content” is the hero image, headline, product card, banner, search result, article title, or another large element in the initial viewport. In other words, LCP is not just a browser metric. It is a signal for whether the user feels the page has loaded. We will cover: • What LCP actually measures • Why lab tools are not enough • What data to collect in production • How to instrument LCP in a frontend app • How to debug LCP using a phase-based approach • What alerts and dashboards should look like • Common frontend mistakes that hurt LCP ⸻ What LCP Measures Largest Contentful Paint measures when the largest visible content element in the viewport finishes rendering. The LCP element is often one of these: • An image • A video poster • A background image • A large heading or paragraph block • A large hero section • A product image • A large article or marketing banner A good LCP target is 2.5 seconds or less for most user visits. Between 2.5 and 4 seconds usually needs improvement. Above 4 seconds is poor. The important phrase here is “most user visits.” You should not judge LCP only by your local machine or a single Lighthouse run. A useful LCP monitoring system looks at the 75th percentile, usually split by mobile and desktop. For example: Press enter or click to view image in full size This tells a much better story than “our LCP is 2.4s.” Performance is not one number. It is a distribution. ⸻ Lab Data vs Field Data There are two broad ways to measure LCP. Lab data is collected in a controlled environment. Tools like Lighthouse, Chrome DevTools, WebPageTest, and local performance profiles are useful because they are repeatable. They help you debug. Field data is collected from real users. This is also called Real User Monitoring, or RUM. Field data captures actual devices, actual networks, actual cache states, actual browser behavior, and actual production conditions. You need both. Lab data helps answer: Why is this page slow in a controlled test? Field data helps answer: Are real users experiencing slow pages in production? For LCP monitoring, field data should be your source of truth. Lab tools are your microscope. A common mistake is to optimize only what Lighthouse shows. That can lead to false confidence. Your test may run on a simulated device, from a specific region, with a specific network profile, without all production traffic patterns. Real users may have slower phones, blocked fonts, longer redirects, colder CDN cache, or heavier personalization. ⸻ What You Should Track for Every LCP Event A basic LCP value is useful, but not enough. If all you collect is this: { "name": "LCP", "value": 3420 }You know the page is slow, but you do not know why. A production-ready LCP event should include enough context to debug the issue later. At minimum, collect: { "metric": "LCP", "value": 3420, "rating": "needs-improvement", "page": "/products/:id", "actualPath": "/products/123", "deviceType": "mobile", "connectionType": "4g", "navigationType": "navigate", "lcpElement": "img.hero-image", "lcpUrl": "https://cdn.example.com/products/123.webp", "ttfb": 620, "resourceLoadDelay": 980, "resourceLoadDuration": 710, "elementRenderDelay": 1110, "timestamp": 1710000000000, "release": "web-2026.06.15", "experiment": "new-product-hero" }This gives you engineering-level visibility. Now you can ask: • Is LCP slow on all pages or only one route? • Is the LCP element an image, text block, or background image? • Is the image discovered late? • Is the server response slow? • Is the resource download slow? • Is JavaScript delaying the render after the resource is already available? • Did a new release or experiment make it worse? Monitoring without attribution is just reporting. Monitoring with attribution becomes debugging. ⸻ A Simple LCP Monitoring Architecture A practical LCP monitoring setup has four parts. 1. Browser instrumentation The frontend app collects LCP using the browser Performance APIs or a library like web-vitals. 2. Analytics endpoint The app sends the event to a lightweight endpoint using navigator.sendBeacon() or fetch() with keepalive. 3. Storage and aggregation The backend stores events in a data store such as BigQuery, ClickHouse, Datadog, Honeycomb, New Relic, Grafana Cloud, or any observability platform. 4. Dashboards and alerts Dashboards show LCP distributions by route, device, release, geography, and LCP element. Alerts fire when P75 or P90 LCP crosses a threshold. The key is to treat performance as production telemetry, not a one-time audit. ⸻ Instrumenting LCP with web-vitals The easiest starting point is the web-vitals package. npm install web-vitalsThen collect LCP: import { onLCP } from 'web-vitals/attribution';function sendMetric(metric: any) { const body = JSON.stringify({ name: metric.name, value: metric.value, rating: metric.rating, id: metric.id, page: location.pathname, url: location.href, attribution: { target: metric.attribution?.target, url: metric.attribution?.url, timeToFirstByte: metric.attribution?.timeToFirstByte, resourceLoadDelay: metric.attribution?.resourceLoadDelay, resourceLoadDuration: metric.attribution?.resourceLoadDuration, elementRenderDelay: metric.attribution?.elementRenderDelay, }, release: window.__APP_RELEASE__, timestamp: Date.now(), }); navigator.sendBeacon('/api/rum', body); } onLCP(sendMetric); The attribution build is especially useful because it tells you more than just the LCP value. It can tell you the LCP target, LCP resource URL, TTFB, resource load delay, resource load duration, and render delay. That is the difference between saying: “Product pages are slow.” and saying: “The product hero image is discovered 1.2 seconds after TTFB because it is loaded through a CSS background image after the stylesheet arrives.” The second statement can be fixed. ⸻ Using LCP in Next.js If your app uses Next.js, you can use useReportWebVitals. Create a small client component: 'use client';import { useReportWebVitals } from 'next/web-vitals'; export function WebVitals() { useReportWebVitals((metric) => { if (metric.name !== 'LCP') return; navigator.sendBeacon( '/api/rum', JSON.stringify({ name: metric.name, value: metric.value, rating: metric.rating, id: metric.id, page: location.pathname, timestamp: Date.now(), }) ); }); return null; } Then import it in your root layout: import { WebVitals } from './components/WebVitals';export default function RootLayout({ children }) { return ( {children} ); } Keep this component small. You do not want your monitoring code to create a large client-side boundary or add meaningful JavaScript cost to every page. ⸻ Debugging LCP by Breaking It into Four Phases A useful way to debug LCP is to split it into four parts: 1. Time to First Byte This is the time from navigation start until the browser receives the first byte of the HTML document. Common causes of high TTFB: • Slow server rendering • Cold serverless starts • Long API calls during SSR • Missing CDN caching • Long redirects • Slow authentication middleware • Backend-for-frontend latency Frontend engineers should care about TTFB because SSR, middleware, personalization, and routing decisions often sit at the frontend/backend boundary. Possible fixes: • Cache HTML where possible • Use CDN and edge caching • Reduce SSR blocking data dependencies • Stream HTML earlier • Avoid unnecessary redirects • Move non-critical personalization after first paint 2. Resource Load Delay This is the delay between receiving the HTML and starting the LCP resource request. This is one of the most common frontend-owned LCP problems. For example, your LCP image may be delayed because: • It is added by JavaScript after hydration • It is hidden behind client-side rendering • It is referenced as a CSS background image • It is lazy-loaded even though it is above the fold • It is inside a carousel that initializes late • It depends on a late API response • The browser discovers it only after downloading CSS or JS Possible fixes: • Put the LCP image directly in the initial HTML • Avoid lazy loading the LCP image • Use fetchpriority=”high” for the hero image • Preload the LCP image when appropriate • Avoid rendering the hero only after client-side JavaScript • Prefer real elements over CSS background images for important content • Make above-the-fold content server-rendered when possible 3. Resource Load Duration This is the actual download time for the LCP resource. Common causes: • Large image files • Unoptimized formats • No responsive image sizes • Poor CDN performance • Missing compression • Serving desktop-sized images to mobile users Possible fixes: • Use AVIF or WebP where appropriate • Use responsive images with srcset and sizes • Resize images at the CDN • Cache images aggressively • Avoid shipping oversized hero images • Use a CDN close to users 4. Element Render Delay This is the time between the LCP resource finishing download and the element actually rendering. This phase often points to main-thread or rendering issues. Common causes: • Heavy JavaScript execution • Hydration blocking paint • Large CSS files • Render-blocking styles • Web font delays • Client-side state initialization • Animation or layout work before showing content Possible fixes: • Reduce startup JavaScript • Split non-critical bundles • Defer third-party scripts • Server-render the LCP content • Avoid hiding the page until hydration completes • Use font-display strategies • Inline only critical CSS • Remove unnecessary above-the-fold layout work The goal is not just “make the image smaller.” The goal is to understand which part of the LCP timeline is wasting time. ⸻ What an LCP Dashboard Should Show A useful dashboard should not be one giant average. Averages hide pain. Use percentiles instead: • P50: typical user experience • P75: Core Web Vitals target view • P90/P95: worst affected users Recommended dashboard sections: • Overall health • P75 LCP by day • P75 LCP by mobile vs desktop • Good / Needs improvement / Poor distribution • LCP trend by release version Route-level breakdown • Home page • Search results • Product detail page • Article page • Checkout page • Dashboard page LCP element breakdown • img.hero • h1.article-title • .product-card img • .marketing-banner • .carousel-slide img Phase breakdown • TTFB • Resource load delay • Resource load duration • Element render delay Business/product breakdown • Logged-in vs logged-out • New vs returning users • Experiment A vs B • Region • Device memory • Network type This helps teams prioritize. If checkout has a 3.2s LCP, that may matter more than a blog page at 3.4s. If mobile product pages regressed after a redesign, that is more actionable than “site LCP is up.” ⸻ Alerting on LCP Without Creating Noise Bad performance alerts are noisy. Good performance alerts are specific. Avoid alerts like: Average LCP is above 2.5s. Prefer alerts like: P75 mobile LCP for /products/:id exceeded 3.0s f
